coredata.dc:title: Conducting polymer encrusted Cu/Mn-MOF-CNDs: A facile synthesis, characterization and its hydrogen evolution activity in alkaline media and seawater
coredata.dc:creator: Pavithra, Suresh
Komal, Murugan
Rajaram, Arulmozhi
Jothi, Vinoth Kumar
Nivetha, Michael raj Sherlin
Natarajan, Abirami
coredata.dc:description: The Composites of polyaniline-bimetallic metal-organic framework with carbon nanodots (PANI/bi-MOF@CNDs) were fabricated by chemically oxidising aniline monomer for the hydrogen evolution process (HER). In this investigation, the composite structure, topography, and characteristics were analysed. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy analyses found evidence of electrostatic interaction between the CNDs@MOF and the backbone of the PANI matrix. Investigations using scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy showed that the CNDs@MOF exhibited uneven crystals with an additional enveloping PANI material. In an alkaline medium, the produced PANI composite (PANI/bi-MOF@CNDs) electrocatalyst showed a small overpotential and significant Tafel slope (110 mV and 180 mV/dec at 10 mA/cm2), as well as the RCT value of about 12.26 Ω, determined from Nyquist plot. PANI composite structural characteristics and electrical conductivity have also resulted in a faster rate of electron charge transfer and more accessible surface edge active sites. In addition, the catalyst is stable for 48 h at the specified operating conditions, and no further buffered electrolytes are required. As a result of the enhanced electrochemical performance, a strategic approach for the development of low-priced electrocatalysts for power conversion has been discovered.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyaniline
Metal-organic framework
Electrocatalyst
Hydrogen evolution reaction

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Special Section on Conducting polymers and their composites for electrochemical energy conversion and storage; Edited by Gordana Ćirić-Marjanović and Igor Pašti

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Barleria cristata leaf extract (BCLE)
Synthesis of N- functionalized carbon nanodots (N-FCNDs)
Development of PANI composite electrode coating and its electrochemical studies

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology , Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu-603 203, India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the preparation of PANI composite.
Synthesis of PANI composite: (a) XRD and (b) FT-IR.
(a, b) FESEM images of CNDs@MOF, PANI composite, (c, d) and (e-i) elemental mapping of PANI composite [C, O, N, Mn, Cu].
(a, b) HRTEM images of PANI composite, (c) EDAX, (d) particle size histogram, (e) lattice fringes, (f) SAED pattern of PANI/MOF composite.
N2 adsorption isotherm of PANI composite (insert: corresponding pore size distribution).
XPS spectrum of PANI composite (a) review spectra, (b) C 1 s, (c) O 1 s, (d) N 1 s, (e) Cu 2p, (f) Mn 2p.

(a) LSV measured at 10 mV/s, (b) Bar graph of overpotential versus Cu/Mn-MOF, CNDs@MOF, and PANI composite (c)Tafel plots, (d) Nyquist plot (EIS) measured at 0.005 V (insert: corresponding equivalent circuit for PANI composite) of Cu/Mn-MOF, CNDs@MOF, and PANI composite in 1 M KOH aqueous solution.
Cyclic voltammetry curves for (a) PANI composite, (b) Cu/Mn-MOF, (c) CNDs@MOF and (b) Current density versus different scan rates.

Durability tests for PANI composite (a) Long-term stability test operated at 10 mA/cm2 in 1.0 M alkaline KOH (chronoamperometry), (b) LSV curves (before and after stability test) and (c) XRD of PANI composite before and after stability test.

(a) LSV polarization curves of PANI composite. (b) Overpotentials of PANI composite in seawater and simulated seawater compared with 1.0 M KOH at 10 mA/cm2, (c) and (d) The stability test of PANI composite in alkaline seawater and simulated electrolytes.
Summary of the HER electrocatalysts with earlier reported PANI coated composites.

head.ce:title: Conducting polymer encrusted Cu/Mn-MOF-CNDs: A facile synthesis, characterization and its hydrogen evolution activity in alkaline media and seawater

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Engineering and Technology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu 603 203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, College of Engineering and Technology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Engineering and Technology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu-603 203, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The Composites of polyaniline-bimetallic metal-organic framework with carbon nanodots (PANI/bi-MOF@CNDs) were fabricated by chemically oxidising aniline monomer for the hydrogen evolution process (HER). In this investigation, the composite structure, topography, and characteristics were analysed. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy analyses found evidence of electrostatic interaction between the CNDs@MOF and the backbone of the PANI matrix. Investigations using scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy showed that the CNDs@MOF exhibited uneven crystals with an additional enveloping PANI material. In an alkaline medium, the produced PANI composite (PANI/bi-MOF@CNDs) electrocatalyst showed a small overpotential and significant Tafel slope (110 mV and 180 mV/dec at 10 mA/cm2), as well as the RCT value of about 12.26 Ω, determined from Nyquist plot. PANI composite structural characteristics and electrical conductivity have also resulted in a faster rate of electron charge transfer and more accessible surface edge active sites. In addition, the catalyst is stable for 48 h at the specified operating conditions, and no further buffered electrolytes are required. As a result of the enhanced electrochemical performance, a strategic approach for the development of low-priced electrocatalysts for power conversion has been discovered.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The continued use of fossil fuels, which causes pollution and climate change, necessitates the development of green and sustainable energy sources [1,2]. Hydrogen production via electrocatalytic water splitting has been demonstrated as a clean and sustainable alternative to finite fossil fuels. It is thought to be a potential strategy for addressing energy shortages and the negative environmental effects of the over usage of hydrocarbon deposits [3]. Water splitting is divided into two half processes: the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) taking place at the cathode and the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) taking place at the anode. The efficiency of water splitting is determined by the catalytic activity of the electrode materials. In particular, the HER in alkaline solution has slower kinetics than that of acidic media, necessitating a large overpotential to promote the reaction process [4]. The additional water dissociation step that generates protons for the succeeding reactions causes the HER to respond slowly in an alkaline solution [5]. However, due to the slow kinetics of HER in alkaline solution and to speed up the process, a high-efficiency electrocatalyst with a low reaction barrier is required. The significant features of an efficient electrocatalyst should contain high porosity, high specific area, more active metal site distribution and more charge transfer capability. Noble metals, such as Ru, Ir and Pt complexes, have shown promise, but their commercial usage has already been restrained due to excessive cost. In terms of advanced material design methodologies, enormous effort has been committed to developing non-noble metal-based electrocatalyst [6].

Non-noble metal-based electrocatalysts have been widely documented as a key component of transition metal electrocatalysts, with outstanding catalytic performance that even outperforms noble-metal-based electrocatalysts [7]. Metal-Organic frameworks (MOFs), which are made up of metal ions or metal ion clusters bound together by organic ligands, have remained at the forefront of materials science study due to their large interior surface areas, high pore size distribution, and tunable environmental stability [8]. Interestingly, the porous aspect of the MOFs structure, as well as the open metal site and the ligands associated with the metals, create an active site and help the HER move forwards [9].

In recent times, carbon-based nanomaterials were also been widely used in oxygen evolution reactions due to their nanometer size, low cost, and stability [10]. The carbon-containing nanomaterials include carbon nitrides, graphene-based compounds, carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbon nanosheets [11]. In these materials, carbon nanodots have unique photoluminescence and catalytic properties [12,13]. In addition to that, it has surface chemistry, more active sites, and high configurable structures which make them interesting for application in successful HER processes. However, when compared to their transition-metal-based counterparts, these catalysts have lower catalytic activity [14]. Barleria cristata is a perennial plant belongs to the family Acanthaceae. It is available in many places and we collected the leaves particularly from the Thiruvalangadu forest area, India. The phytochemical constituents present in Barleria cristata leaves are alkaloids, proteins, carbohydrates, flavonoids, phytosterols, tannins, terpenes, steroids, phenols and amino acids [15]. Due to the availability of rich phytochemicals, it is used as a novel carbon precursor for synthesizing carbon nanodots. The elevation of the pant is from 35 to 50 in.. The blooming time (season) of this plant is from September to December. The plant has antioxidants, chemicals that kill cells, and chemicals that kill germs [16]. In this current study, we showed that the carbon dots can be prepared from aqueous leaf extract of the Barleria cristata, which is cheap and good for the environment and not been reported yet.

Polyaniline (PANI) as a component of composite materials has sparked a lot of interest in the field of energy storage and conversion due to its superior geographies, such as high environmental stability, low cost, ease of synthesis, increased electrical conductivity, and magnetic redox properties connected to the chain nitrogen [17]. PANI, on the other hand, by itself is extremely unreliable in electrocatalytic applications because of the substantial charge transformation it undergoes. It's possible that the development of composites with other metals or metal oxides could help attenuate this effect. As a result, the combination of the properties of a conducting polymer and an inorganic metal oxide may aid in the development of a new category of materials with superior properties that can outperform their equivalents. Recent research has shown that the nitrogen component of polyaniline (PANI) can readily trap H+ from dissolved ions to form protonated NH2 groups. This allows for an increased surface charge density of H+ on the surface of the electrode, resulting in rapid kinetics, a low overpotential, and outstanding catalytic output for HER in acidic media [18,19].

Herein we synthesized polyaniline coated on a bimetallic organic framework composite (CNDs@MOF) using a simple polymerization method. The intrinsic activity, the number of active sites, and the electrical conductivity concerns can all be handled simultaneously in the simplified preparation method of this efficient and cost-effective catalyst. As a consequence of this, the non-precious PANI composite electrocatalyst demonstrated unusually high activity and outstanding stability toward the HER in alkaline solutions, which is comparable to the performance of the standard Pt/C catalyst.

Barleria cristata were collected from the Thiruvalangadu forest area, copper (II) nitrate hexahydrate [Cu (NO3)2.6 H2O], Manganese (II) acetate (C4H6MnO4), terephthalic acid (TPA), aniline, ammonium persulfate (APS), acetylene black and polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) was purchased from Avra chemicals. Sigma-Aldrich marketed solvents such as C2H5OH, N, N-Dimethyl formamide (DMF), and NH3. Vijaya Scientifics was used to purchase potassium hydroxide (KOH). For every experiment in this study, we used double distilled water. Nickel foam substrates were ordered from Thermo-fisher Scientifics. Every single reagent used in this experiment was of analytical purity and did not require further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Barleria cristata leaf extract (BCLE)
ce:section.ce:para: Barleria cristata leaves were taken, cleaned with double deionized water, and desiccated and crush into powder form. The powder was stirred with double deionized water with the use of a magnetic stirrer for about 3 h under room temperature. After that, the resulting solution is filtered using Whatman filter paper and the filtered liquid is stored safely to prepare N-CNDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N- functionalized carbon nanodots (N-FCNDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) were synthesised using the hydrothermal technique by leaf extract of Barleria cristata. Typically, 20 ml of leaf extract with 1 ml of an ammonia solution was added into a 100 ml stainless steel autoclave and heated to 170 οC for 9 h. After that, the final product was centrifuged for 15 min at 4500 rpm. The N-CNDs containing supernatant liquid was collected and stored.

The bimetallic metal-organic framework was prepared by a one-step solvothermal method. The mixture of 1 g of copper (II) nitrate hexahydrate [Cu (NO3)20.6 H2O] and 2 g of terephthalic acid (TPA) dissolved in 25 ml of N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF) is ultrasonically stirred for 5 mins at room temperature. To the completely dissolved solution, 1 g of Manganese (II) acetate (C4H6MnO4) was added under magnetic stirring. The completely dissolved mixture is then transferred to a 100 ml Teflon-lined steel autoclave. It is then placed in the hot-air oven at 130 οC for about 26 h. The obtained blue colour solution is then washed using ethanol and distilled water several times. The precipitate is dried at 60 οC for 12 h in an oven.

About 1.3 g of terephthalic acid (TPA) is dissolved in 40 ml ethanol in a 100 ml beaker. On the other hand, 0.6 g of Cu salt and 0.6 g of Mn salt was dissolved in 40 ml of distilled water. Under magnetic stirring, the salt solutions were rapidly poured into the TPA ethanolic acid solution. Without any further delay, 2 ml of N functionalized CNDs solution is added to the above solution under stirring. The reaction was carried under room temperature at constant stirring for 1 hr, then the resultant solution was then poured into an autoclave and heated to 180 οC for 8 h in an oven. The resultant precipitate was then washed numerous using either ethanol or methanol. The product obtained was pale blue and it is dried in an oven at 60 οC for 4 h.

To create a PANI composite, a chemical oxidation polymerization process was used, in which 0.25 M distilled aniline monomer was combined with 20 ml 1 M aqueous HCl to make the initial solution. After 30 min of stirring at room temperature, anilinium ions were produced. Another solution was made by combining 0.2 M APS with 30 ml of 1 M HCl and slowly adding it to the first followed by the addition of already prepared CNDs@MOF composite. This solution was continually stirred for several hours until green precipitates appeared. These green precipitates were then washed twice with DI water and three to four times with acetone before being dried for 12 h at 60 οC. In the end, dark greenish colour particles were seen, indicating the synthesis of PANI in the emeraldine phase. The above Fig.1 shows a schematic representation of the preparation of PANI composite.

PXRD measurements were recorded utilising a PAN analytical X'pert pro diffractometer equipped at 40 kV and 20 mA with Cu Kα radiation. Utilizing an FEI Tecnai F20 and Hitachi HT7700, the topology of synthesized samples was examined. Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) analysis was performed in ALPHA-T-FT-IR Spectrometer. A 100 kV JEOL model 1010 microscope was used to obtain transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. Experiments involving X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) utilised the MgK source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of PANI composite electrode coating and its electrochemical studies
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical performances of samples were carried out using a Biologic electrochemical workstation in an aqueous solution containing 1 M KOH under a three-electrode system at room temperature. The nickel foam contains a composite material as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference, and a platinum electrode as the counter electrode, respectively. The working electrode is composed of MOF as the active material (75 w%), PVDF as the binder (10 w%), and acetylene black as the pigment (15 w%) all suspended in N Methyl-2-Pyrrolidone (NMP) as the solvent. To achieve the homogenous slurry, the mixture is ground using a mortar and pestle. This makes it possible to readily cover nickel foam with the slurry. The contaminants were removed from the nickel foam by performing a thorough precleaning process using acid, acetone, ethanol, and DI water. After that, the homogeneous slurry was applied to an area of nickel foam measuring 1 × 0.5 cm2, and the mass-loaded was approximately 2.8 mg.

The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) method was utilised to evaluate all the electrocatalytic performances with a scan rate of 10 mV/s. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, also known as EIS, was carried out throughout a frequency spectrum ranging from 1 Hz to 105 Hz.

The equation ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.198 V + 0.059 pH was used to determine the potentials that were measured in this investigation, and these potentials were referred to as a reversible hydrogen electrode or RHE [20].

To substantiate the purity of the synthetic MOF, an XRD analysis was performed. Fig. 2(a) depicts the XRD patterns of PANI composite, CNDs@MOF, and Cu/Mn-MOF. The diffraction peaks of CNDs@MOF at 18 ο, 26.2 ο, 28.9 ο, 32.3 ο, 36.0 ο and 44.4 ο correspond to (101), (002), (112), (103), (211) and (220) planes respectively which indicates the presence of copper in + 2 oxidation state and manganese in mixed oxidation states. This also indicated that CNDs is incorporated into MOF structure hinting that MOF structure is affected by CNDs [21,22]. Diffraction peaks of the PANI/MOF composite are similar to those of the plain PANI which is already reported in previous literature, but there are no visible diffraction peaks of MOF [23]. This suggests that PANI's amorphous structure is retained in MOF composites.

The nature of functional groups in the Cu/Mn-MOF, N-CNDs@MOF and PANI composite can be determined by the FT-IR spectroscopy Fig.2(b). The synthesized Cu/Mn-MOF showed characteristic bands at 1543 cm-1 indicating the stretching vibrations of COO- groups and a strong peak in 1388 cm-1 confirms the C-N bond attributed to the -COO ligand [24]. The peak at 1024 cm-1 and 925 cm-1 indicates the weak δ(C-H) vibration of phenyl rings and the presence of C-O bonds [25]. The peaks noted between 500 and 750 cm-1 donated the occurrence of metal oxide bonds ( Mn-O & Cu-O), which suggests the effective coordination of the metal ions to the organic linker [26]. The synthesized CNDs@MOF showed bands at 1572 cm-1 confirming the COO- stretching vibration and 608 cm-1 due to the presence of ring deformation in the phenyl ring which indicates that the carbon dots are well incorporated in the MOF structure [27]. The PANI/MOF composite adsorption peak corresponds to the conducting PANI emeraldine salt form at 1118 cm-1 corresponds to the presence of the (=N+·–H) group [28]. This information indicates that PANI not only has physical absorption with N-CNDs but also has further strong electrostatic interactions with the MOF.

Scanning electron microscopy is utilised to look at the morphology and structural integrity of the sample. The preliminary assessment of the samples reveals the three-dimensional morphology of the materials that were produced. This material demonstrates 3D crystals in the shape of rods, rectangles, and squares. The SEM morphology of the MOF in its natural state is shown in Fig.3(a, b). By using a polymerization method, the resulting MOF was subsequently combined with PANI. Fig.3(c, d) displays the many morphologies that can be seen in the doped material. According to these findings, PANI and MOF combine to generate a matrix formation that offers a significant number of active sites for charge collection and storage. Concurrently, the blending process works to reduce the particle size of the mixture. The doped material morphology provides evidence that confirms that the PANI is adorned on the MOF structures during the polymerization process. The construction of this matrix is advantageous for the high activity as well as the high conductivity and the high specific capacity [29]. In addition, an energy dispersive x-ray diffraction pattern was utilised to investigate the elemental consistency. The Fig. 3(e-i) explains the elemental composition of the PANI composite, which indicates the presence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, manganese, and copper.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was utilised in order to investigate and quantify the PANI composite's appearance as well as its structural properties. The TEM image, shown in Fig. 4(a,b), depict a rectangular structure with non-uniform dimensions and shape. Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping profiles are displayed in Fig. 4(c). These images provide evidence that the catalyst contains the elements carbon, oxygen, copper, manganese, and nitrogen in the catalyst as well as the effective doping of PANI (conducting polymer). Fig. 4(d) shows the particle size distribution in which the average particle size of the PANI composite is 2.6 nm. The HRTEM image shown in Fig. 3(e) reveals that the composite is in amorphous in nature with disordered fringes. The SAED pattern shows Fig. 4(f) that the synthesized PANI composite has amorphous in nature [30]. Recent research has shown that amorphous materials may be better than their crystalline counterparts in terms of electrochemical activity and overpotential values. Surface flaws and a lot of intrinsic defects are responsible for better electrocatalytic properties. These may provide more active sites to get better electrochemical properties [31]. The TEM images for N-CNDs and its size distribution was described in figure S1.

To determine the sample's surface area and pore-size distribution, N2-adsorption/desorption measurements were performed shown in Fig. (5). It can be deduced from the fact that the PANI composites exhibited an IV-type isotherm with P/P0 greater than 0.4 that the PANI composites contain mesopores. The pore size distribution of these samples is primarily centred at 2–10 nm, which indicates the presence of mesopores. The PANI composite's high surface area and rich mesoporous structure benefit from HER active sites [32]. Due to its greater pore radius (15.311 Å), high surface area (59.246 m2/g) and pore volume (0.131 cc/g), the PANI composite offers rapid electrolyte access through pores easily and helps to boost HER activity.

The surface chemical composition of the PANI composite may very well be revealed by the XPS measurements. The XPS survey spectrum of the PANI composite were displayed in Fig.6(a). These spectra demonstrated that the sample consists of the elements C, O, N, Cu, and Mn. It was revealed that the atomic percentages of the following elements were present: C accounts for 60.2 %, O for 15.4 %, N for 3.4 %, Cu for 12.6 %, and Mn for 8.4 % of the total. In addition to this, the narrow spectra which correspond to C 1 s, O 1 s, N1s, Cu 2p, and Mn 2p were deconvoluted such that an estimate could be made on the degree to which the various states of each element are present in the PANI composite. Fig.6(b) demonstrates that the C1s XPS spectra had a prominent peak at the energy level of 284.7 eV, which corresponded to sp2 hybridised C–C bonds [33]. In addition, the C1s XPS spectra displayed two peaks, one at 286.0 eV and the other at 288.0 eV, and each of these peaks might be attributed to either C–O bonds or CO bonds [34]. The O 1 s spectra shown in Fig. 6c can be slitted into three peaks, which have a binding energy of 529.7, 531.5, and 532.9 eV respectively. These energies are respectively assigned to the M–O, O–C, and absorbed H2O molecules (M-Cu, Mn) [35]. The sharp peak shown in Fig.6(d) in N1s spectra at 401.9 eV denotes the presence of graphitic nitrogen. The binding energies at 398.8 and 400.0 eV, respectively, were assigned to N atoms bonded with sp3 -hybridized C atoms and N atoms bonded with sp2-hybridized C atoms [36]. The XPS spectra (Fig. 6(e) showed that there were two peaks of Cu, one with a binding energy of 934.9 eV and the other at a binding energy of 955.0 eV. These two peaks matched Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively. In the region of 940–950 eV, a shake-up satellite peak was seen, which provided more evidence that copper existed in the PANI composite in the form of Cu2+ [37]. Fig. 6(f) illustrates that the Mn 2p spectra include two distinctive peaks at 641.5 and 653.3 eV, which can be attributed to Mn (II) 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively. The Mn 2p3/2 was further deconvoluted into three primary peaks, as shown in Fig. 6f. These peaks correspond to the mixed valance states of the manganese oxide and are located at binding energies of 640.9, 642.5, and 645.2 eV, respectively [38].

To determine the electrocatalytic activity of Cu/Mn-MOF, CNDs@MOF and PANI composite in alkaline conditions, we first performed electrochemical experiments in 1 M KOH. Fig.7(a) depicts the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of the tested catalysts in 1 M KOH at 10 mV/s, as measured by the LSV technique.

The main role of N-functionalized carbon nanodots is to increase the conductivity of the composite material (Cu/Mn-MOF) and provide a large surface area to accumulate the charge, which contributes to an increase in the hydrogen evolution concentration. The nitrogen content in CNDs is responsible for the larger density of exposed active sites and improvement of the catalytic activity of CNDs, as the neighbouring carbon atoms are converted into active sites. Particularly, nitrogen containing moieties could combine with protons and favour the formation of carbonaceous composites with myriad active sites. This not only enhances the catalytic properties meritoriously but also hampers the corrosion of catalyst affording good stability [20]. In addition to that we are coating the MOF composite with conducting polymer to further increase the catalytic activity. These are the main reasons for using the N functionalized carbon nanodots. The electrocatalytic performance for N-CNDs was measured with an overpotential of 275 mV at 10 mA/cm2 (Figure S3). PANI composite catalyst has significantly better electrocatalytic behaviour than Cu/Mn-MOF and CNDs@MOF. This research reveals that the inclusion of PANI into bimetallic MOF composite (CNDs@Cu/Mn-MOF) increases the HER activity in the alkaline solution. PANI composite has a lower overpotential of 110 mV when subjected to 10 mA/cm2, which is evidence of the outstanding HER activity it possesses. This is because the surface of the nickel foam allows for the easy transmission of electronic charges. Another reason for the better catalytic activity of the PANI composite may be due to the synergistic effect between the MOF, CNDs and PANI. This also plays a major role in the performance of the electrocatalyst. The overpotentials (ƞ) for the PANI composite, CNDs@MOF, and Cu/Mn-MOF electrodes were 0.110 V, 0.146 V, & 0.161 V, respectively, at a current density of 10 mA/cm2 Fig. 7(b).

HER electrocatalytic kinetic performances were studied using Tafel plot analysis. To calculate the Tafel slope value, the following formula was used,
(1) ƞ= a + b log j0
where 'a' is the intercept, 'j0' is the current density, and 'b' is the slope of the Tafel plot [39].

The Tafel slope displayed in Fig. 7(c) for the PANI composite is moderate and demonstrates its remarkable electrocatalytic behaviour. The Tafel value was determined to be 180 mV/dec. When compared to the slope values of Cu/Mn-MOF (261 mV/dec) and CNDs@MOF (230 mV/dec), this value is significantly low. These results demonstrate about the HER catalytic activity of the PANI-coated MOF composite is superior to that of other currently available catalysts. This is owing to the increased catalyst surface and multi-layered porous structure, which allows for a large active surface area and enables gaseous product flow [40]. It is usually believed that HER in alkaline solutions follows either the Volmer-Heyrovsky Eqs. (2) and (3) or Volmer-Tafel Eqs. (2) and (4) or a mixture of both pathways. Tafel slope values predict the mechanism used by HER on a specific surface. The predicted mechanism of hydrogen volution reaction on the surface of a tailored PANI composite coating is Volmer- Heyrovsky, with the Heyrovsky step dictating the overall rate of the reaction. The HER is a process that involves a two-electron transfer reaction, and to lower the energy barriers that are present in each step, active catalysts are necessary to be present. The commonly accepted HER pathways in the alkaline medium are represented as follows: [41].
(2) Volmer step: Metal + H2O + e-
→ Metal-H* + OH-
(3) Heyrovsky step: H2O + e- + Metal-H* → H2 + OH-
(4) Tafel step: Metal-H* + Metal-H* → H2 + Metal

We carried out potential applied electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to gain a deeper comprehension of the electrode kinetics of the developed electrode materials which is shown in Fig. 7(d). For the CNDs@MOF, the diameter of the semicircle produced is smaller than for the Cu/Mn-MOF, indicating a faster electron charge transfer that allows for a simple and active electron transfer via the composite coating. This was demonstrated by the fact that the diameter of the semicircle dropped. The diameter of the semi-circle decreased more progressively in the PANI composite, indicating higher electrocatalytic activity and quicker charge transport. The relevant equivalent electrical circuit (Fig: 7(d) insert) for PANI composite EIS spectra consists of an electrochemical charge transfer resistance (Rct) connected in with a parallel capacitor (C). The charge-transfer resistance value of the composite was lower having a value of about 12.26 Ω (RCT) having capacitance value of145.6 µF and a smaller semicircle diameter than the MOF, CNDs@MOF, and PANI composites, indicating faster electron charge transfer through PANI composite. The corresponding equivalent circuits with their EIS spectra for Cu/Mn-MOF and CNDs@Cu/Mn-MOF were described in the supporting information (Figure S4). These findings indisputably demonstrated that covering MOF with PANI greatly reduced resistance and improved the electronic conductivity of the composite material, enabling quicker charge transfer [42].

The double-layer capacitance (Cdl) test is an additional significant auxiliary tool that is used in the search for possible catalysts for HER. This test is performed to assess the electrochemically active surface (ESCA) of the manufactured catalyst. The capacitance value of the catalyst determines the relationship between the electrochemically active surface area of the catalyst and the capacitance value. Cdl can be determined through the use of a non-faradaic current area of the cyclic voltammetry runs that were recorded at various scan rates. This method is known as the cyclic voltammetry method. The current density of non-faradaic anodic and cathodic zones, denoted by Ja and Jc, respectively, were utilised in the calculation of ΔJ [1/4(Ja-Jc)], which is then displayed against the scan rate. The Cdl value can be determined by calculating the slope of the linear graph that compares ΔJ to the scan rate. If the double-layer capacitance has a greater value, this indicates that the catalyst has a larger electrochemically active surface area [43]. The cyclic voltammetry technique was taken at different scan rates of 50, 75, 100 and 125 mV/s respectively to calculate the Cdl value for PANI composite, Cu/Mn-MOF and CNDs@MOF shown in Fig. 8(a, b&c). These findings suggest that the ideal electrocatalytic HER performance of the PANI composite may be connected to a higher ESCA, which is beneficial to the electrode-electrolyte interfacial region and indicates that there are more binding sites on the surface. This conclusion was reached as a result of the observations. It was observed that the electrochemical double-layer capacitance values (Cdl) for Cu/Mn-MOF, CNDs@MOF, and PANI composite were 28.4 mF, 42.7 mF, and 47.6 mF respectively (Fig. 8d). It was determined that the ECSA is directly proportional to the value of Cdl [44]. The fact that the PANI composite has more surface-active binding sites, which could have improved its electrocatalytic activity, is the primary part of this work. A slurry was used to coat the composite material that was then applied to Ni foam, which is a porous material that contains both macropores and mesopores. The nickel foam's macropores were helpful in dissipating gaseous by-products and facilitating the mass transfer of ions. The mechanism of electrocatalytic HER in an alkaline medium involves three reactions. The amount of PANI composite in the coating was optimised to control the rate of HER on the coating surface. The tailored composite coating displays distinct HER features because of the higher number of binding sites.

The PANI composite offered a significant number of active surface sites that were easily accessible, along with channels that were suitable for ion diffusion. The value of the overpotential of the obtained PANI composite at 10 mA/cm2 was significantly better than that of several other PANI-based electrocatalysts that had been reported earlier were shown in Table 1.

In addition to this, the long-term stability of the electrocatalyst is a crucial characteristic to consider while analysing the electrocatalyst. Chronoamperometry is an established method of amperometry. Using this technique, a working electrode is given a pulsed potential, and the current flowing across the cell is measured about the passage of time [48]. An alteration in the current can be seen in response to a change in the analyte's diffuse layer thickness on the working electrode. According to the IUPAC's definition, the diffused layer is the area around an electrode where analyte concentrations are differing from those present in the bulk solution [49]. The long-lasting stability test of PANI composite for 48 h at a constant potential of 0.110 V (Vs RHE) is shown in Fig. 9(a). Before and after the stability test, the LSV polarization curves in the 1 M KOH electrolyte show a small difference in overpotential, which is rather impressive Fig. 9(b). In addition, XRD was performed on the electrodes prior to and after the stability test, wherein the graphitic peak of polyaniline was identified as shown in Fig. 9(c). However, catalysts were relatively stable for up to twenty-four hours without any degradation. These results shows that the polymer encrusted MOF was stable due to the combined physical and chemical properties of MOF and PANI which provides excellent catalytic stability.

To further widen the practical application of PANI composite, it’s HER activity was further investigated in simulated seawater and alkaline seawater electrolytes. This was done in order to further expand the scope of PANI composite's potential applications. The three LSV curves of PANI composite exhibit more or less the same behaviour regardless of whether Cl is available or not because of the extremely low overpotential that is below 480 mV, which isn't large enough to activate the ClOR to accomplish 100 % electrolysis of seawater, as shown in Fig. 10(a) [50]. This is because the PANI composite has an extremely low overpotential. Additionally, the PANI composite catalyst's HER activity still functions effectively with only a slight decrease in the actual saltwater electrolyte, requiring merely 185 mV to produce 10 mA/cm2. It is common knowledge that the source of the decline in catalyst activity is inextricably linked to the dynamic environment of real salt water. During the operation of the HER, various species that are present in real seawater, such as microorganisms, and organic and inorganic impurities, might induce catalyst poisoning, and develop insoluble dense precipitates over the catalyst surface [51]. This causes some binding sites to be unable to play a significant catalytic role. Despite this, PANI composite can generate the current density of 10 mA/cm2 for 6 h in the simulated (1.0 M KOH + 0.5 M NaCl) and 24 h in alkaline salt water (1.0 M KOH + seawater) electrolytes without considerable attenuation as shown in Fig. 10(c&d). This indicates that the chloride-containing species in the electrolyte have no appreciable toxic effect on the catalytic sites, which is mostly due to the better performance of PANI composite.

In summary, the PANI enveloped bimetallic metal-organic framework with carbon nanodots was synthesized through a simple polymerization method. In an alkaline environment, the combined effects of a unique porous structure and multiple metal sites in a PANI composite (PANI/bi-MOF@CNDs) system make it possible to achieve an advanced level of electrocatalytic activity with a relatively low overpotential of 110 mV while maintaining a current density of 10 mA/cm2. According to the results of the EIS and Tafel slope measurements, the existence of PANI on the surface of the electrocatalyst affected the process of reaction, which resulted in increased catalytic activity at higher particle contents in the composite. The PANI composite is a potential catalyst for a practical renewable energy application because to its favourable electrochemical metrics and its easy and inexpensive synthesis.

Suresh Pavithra: Writing – original draft, Murugan Komal: Data curation, Arulmozhi Rajaram: Formal Analysis, Vinoth Kumar Jothi: Formal Analysis, Michael raj Sherlin Nivetha: Formal Analysis, Abirami Natarajan: Conceptualization, Investigation & Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu-603 203, India.
